title: French almond praline
subtitle: Dry caramel with whole almonds, broken into shards
excerpt: Classic French pralin: blanched almonds coated in a deep amber dry caramel, poured onto paper and left to set hard. Break into shards to eat, or pulse to a coarse powder for use in cakes, creams, and ice cream. About 400 g.
sourceNotes: French almond praline (pralin) is attributed to the household of Marshal du Plessis-Praslin in the seventeenth century. Pre-1928 recipes appear in Marie-Antoine Careme, Le Patissier Royal Parisien (1815, public domain), and in pre-1928 French confectionery manuals available through the Bibliotheque nationale de France digital archives. The dry caramel method for praline is consistent across all pre-1928 French confectionery sources.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight container for 2 weeks. Avoid humidity, which will make the caramel sticky.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Stovetop only. Dry sugar caramel target: around 175°C (deep amber stage). A sugar thermometer helps but an experienced cook can judge by colour: deep amber, not pale gold, not dark brown.

glossaryTerms.definition: A caramel made by melting sugar directly in the pan without added water, as opposed to a wet caramel where water is added first to dissolve the sugar before boiling. Dry caramel melts faster and more unevenly, requiring constant attention and gentle swirling rather than stirring. Use a heavy-based pan over medium heat to avoid hot spots. The target for praline is deep amber: pale gold is under-cooked and tastes flat; dark brown is over-cooked and tastes bitter. Once the nuts are added the mixture cools rapidly, so the pour onto the lined tray must be immediate.

content.text: French praline is a two-ingredient confection: almonds and caramel. The method is the
method, which requires attention but no special equipment beyond a heavy pan. The result sets into amber shards. Eat as a confection or crush for use in creams, cakes, and ice cream. About 400 g.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. Warm the almonds in a dry pan over low heat for 5 to 6 minutes, stirring, until lightly golden and fragrant. Tip onto the lined tray and set aside within easy reach. The tray must be ready before you start the caramel.

Spread the caster sugar in an even layer in a heavy-based saucepan. Cook over medium heat without stirring. When the edges begin to melt and turn golden, swirl the pan gently to move the unmelted sugar into the liquid. Continue until all the sugar has melted to a deep amber. Working quickly, tip in the warm almonds and the salt if using, and stir with a heatproof spatula to coat. Pour immediately onto the lined tray and spread as thinly as possible. Leave untouched for 30 minutes until fully set and hard. Break into shards with your hands or the back of a heavy spoon.
